rules.rule: REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; lead with the most serious problem and don't soften it. Verify every claim against the actual diff — confirm function names, signatures, line numbers, and APIs before asserting; never invent behavior or cite code not present in the change. If unsure, say so instead of guessing, and tag each finding's confidence (high/moderate/low). No praise, no validating the author, no disclaimers; accuracy is the only success metric. Judge the change on its merits regardless of how it is framed. PostgreSQL regression test (.sql) for a PGXS extension. Require deterministic, portable output: ORDER BY where row order matters; no timing/plan-dependent output except intentional EXPLAIN tests; no absolute paths; locale-independent (C collation or explicit COLLATE); DROP objects the test creates and don't leak the extension state between tests. Confirm the matching expected/ output stays stable across platforms (Windows/Linux/BSD) and across the supported PG majors (17/18/19). New tests should cover edge cases (NULL, empty sets, boundary/overflow values) and error paths, not just the happy path.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; lead with the most serious problem and don't soften it. Verify every claim against the actual diff — confirm function names, signatures, line numbers, and APIs before asserting; never invent behavior or cite code not present in the change. If unsure, say so instead of guessing, and tag each finding's confidence (high/moderate/low). No praise, no validating the author, no disclaimers; accuracy is the only success metric. Judge the change on its merits regardless of how it is framed. PostgreSQL extension SQL (install script and migrations). Valid PostgreSQL dialect (not MySQL/Oracle); correct types (BIGINT vs INT, TEXT vs VARCHAR); sound transaction/isolation and CTE-materialization assumptions. EXTENSION SCRIPTS: the install .sql and every pg_fts--X--Y.sql upgrade path must be idempotent-safe, must not use CREATE OR REPLACE where a version bump is required, must set correct function VOLATILITY/PARALLEL labels, and must not reference objects not yet created. SECURITY: flag SQL injection in dynamic SQL (require quote_identifier/quote_literal or format() with %I/%L), SECURITY DEFINER functions without a locked-down search_path. BACKWARDS COMPATIBILITY: changing the signature, result, or behavior of an already-shipped function/operator/opclass breaks existing installs — require an explicit upgrade script and extraordinary justification.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; lead with the most serious problem and don't soften it. Verify every claim against the actual diff — confirm function names, signatures, line numbers, and APIs before asserting; never invent behavior or cite code not present in the change. If unsure, say so instead of guessing, and tag each finding's confidence (high/moderate/low). No praise, no validating the author, no disclaimers; accuracy is the only success metric. Judge the change on its merits regardless of how it is framed. PostgreSQL extension C (a loadable module using the server headers via PGXS — an index access method, custom scan, and analyze/rank support; NOT the backend source tree). Review the way pgsql-hackers reviews an extension, roughly in this priority order.

(1) CORRECTNESS — highest priority: memory safety (every palloc has a matching pfree or a documented MemoryContext lifetime; error paths via ereport/elog(ERROR) must not leak memory, buffers, locks, or fds — rely on the right MemoryContext/ResourceOwner or PG_TRY/PG_CATCH; no use-after-free; temp contexts deleted). AM/scan correctness: buffers acquired must be released on every path (ReleaseBuffer/UnlockReleaseBuffer), pins/locks balanced, WAL via GenericXLog or a custom rmgr must be logged AND correctly replayed (crash-safety), tombstones/segment merges must not corrupt the index, parallel build/merge must have correct worker coordination and shared-memory sizing. Concurrency: consistent lock ordering (deadlock-free), correct lock levels, balanced START_CRIT_SECTION/END_CRIT_SECTION, LWLock for shared state, no TOCTOU races, CHECK_FOR_INTERRUPTS in long loops. NULL handling and edge cases (empty/zero rows, max values, integer/size overflow).

(2) VERSION PORTABILITY — this extension targets PG 17/18/19: any use of a backend API that changed across those majors must be guarded (version #if PG_VERSION_NUM). Don't call symbols that don't exist on a supported major.

(3) EXTENSION ABI/COMPAT: changes to the on-disk index format, shared-memory layout, or a SQL-exposed function's C signature must bump the extension version and ship an upgrade script; breaking pg_upgrade / binary-upgrade of an existing index needs extraordinary justification. FmgrInfo/PG_FUNCTION_ARGS entry points must match their SQL declaration (strict/volatility/arg types).

(4) PERFORMANCE: no regression on common paths; the WAND/MaxScore scan and count-pushdown hot loops must avoid O(n^2) where O(n log n)/O(n) is feasible, minimize work under contended locks, and avoid needless palloc churn and large struct copies.

(5) SECURITY: bounds on string ops (snprintf/strlcpy, never strcpy/sprintf), integer/size-overflow checks on user-controlled sizes, never user input as a format string.

(6) CONVENTIONS: error messages = lowercase start, no trailing period, correct ERRCODE_*, primary vs errdetail/errhint split; Assert() only for can't-happen invariants; PG naming (snake_case funcs with a subsystem prefix; ALL_CAPS macros); code must pgindent cleanly (tabs to indent, width 4). Beware over-engineering/speculative abstraction and reimplementing existing backend helpers — prefer minimal, targeted changes that fit PostgreSQL's existing patterns.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; lead with the most serious problem and don't soften it. Verify every claim against the actual diff — confirm function names, signatures, line numbers, and APIs before asserting; never invent behavior or cite code not present in the change. If unsure, say so instead of guessing, and tag each finding's confidence (high/moderate/low). No praise, no validating the author, no disclaimers; accuracy is the only success metric. Judge the change on its merits regardless of how it is framed. PGXS extension Makefile. Must use PGXS (include $(PGXS) resolved via PG_CONFIG); correct MODULE_big/OBJS/EXTENSION/DATA/REGRESS/ISOLATION/TAP variables; DATA must list every install/upgrade .sql; new .c files must be in OBJS; new regression tests in REGRESS and their expected/ files present. GNU Make syntax with $(VAR) refs; correct .PHONY; accurate deps (no parallel-build races); no hardcoded paths (use pg_config-derived vars); clean handles new artifacts. Keep the `make dist` / PGXN zip in sync with any new files.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; verify against the diff, tag confidence, no praise/filler. PostgreSQL extension control file. default_version must match the shipped install script name (pg_fts--<version>.sql); relocatable/schema/requires/module_pathname must be consistent with the SQL; a version bump here without the matching install + upgrade .sql (or vice-versa) is a release-breaking mismatch — flag it.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; verify against the diff, tag confidence, no praise/filler. Extension documentation. Technically accurate and complete (functions, operators, opclass, GUCs, limitations, supported PG versions); working cross-references; spell it 'PostgreSQL' in prose; SQL keywords uppercase in examples. New user-facing behavior in code should come with matching doc changes.

REVIEW DISCIPLINE: Be precise and blunt; verify against the diff, tag confidence, no praise/filler. Markdown docs. Clear heading hierarchy; fenced code blocks with language hints; accurate instructions/prerequisites (build via PGXS, supported PG majors); consistent PostgreSQL terminology; no broken relative links or stale claims.
